pathophysiology_summary: Pityriasis rosea is a common, benign, self-limiting papulosquamous eruption widely attributed to reactivation of human herpesviruses HHV-6/HHV-7, which explains the mild viral prodrome two weeks earlier. It classically begins with a solitary 'herald patch' (a single larger scaly plaque) followed 1-2 weeks later by a symmetrical eruption of smaller oval salmon-coloured macules and papules distributed along skin cleavage (Langer's) lines on the trunk, producing the characteristic 'Christmas tree' pattern on the back, each lesion showing an inward-facing collarette of fine scale. It spares the face, palms and soles, and resolves spontaneously over 6-8 weeks with possible transient post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation but no scarring. The diagnostic task is not difficulty but discrimination and reassurance: the florid, rapid, frightening appearance mimics secondary syphilis (which characteristically DOES involve palms and soles), guttate psoriasis, widespread tinea, and a drug eruption. The strict absence of palm/sole involvement, absence of systemic toxicity, absence of any new medication, and the herald-patch-then-Christmas-tree evolution make pityriasis rosea the clear diagnosis and exclude the dangerous mimics clinically.

differential.evidence_basis: A single larger patch preceding a rapid widespread scaly truncal eruption in a well young adult is the classic herald-patch-then-eruption pattern.
A widespread maculopapular rash in a young adult must prompt active exclusion of secondary syphilis until palm/sole involvement and risk are checked.
A rapid widespread eruption warrants a medication history to exclude a drug reaction.
Post-streptococcal/viral guttate psoriasis can present with an abrupt scaly truncal eruption in a young adult.
Scaly annular lesions raise tinea, but a sudden diffuse eruption after a solitary patch is atypical for tinea.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Even on the alarming opening framing, the AI Doctor should treat pityriasis rosea as leading while explicitly holding secondary syphilis as important-not-to-miss — it must not be spooked into over-calling a serious systemic illness, nor prematurely dismiss the syphilis mimic before checking palms/soles.

differential.evidence_basis: Herald patch + Christmas-tree truncal distribution + preceding viral prodrome + mild pruritus in a systemically well patient.
Still possible on distribution, but the herald patch and collarette (inward) scaling favour pityriasis rosea over whole-lesion micaceous psoriatic scale.
A sudden diffuse eruption rather than slowly expanding annular rings with central clearing argues against tinea.

differential_progression.scoring_note: With the herald-patch history and viral prodrome elicited, pityriasis rosea should now be clearly leading and psoriasis/tinea downgraded.

differential.evidence_basis: Palms and soles spared, no systemic toxicity, no new medication — the dangerous mimics are excluded clinically.
Strict sparing of palms and soles and absence of mucosal lesions/systemic features exclude secondary syphilis clinically (serology only if sexual-risk factors emerge).
No new prescription, OTC or recreational medication in the preceding weeks.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The negative screen (palms/soles spared, no systemic toxicity, no new drug) is what converts a frightening-looking rash into a confident benign diagnosis — the AI Doctor should reassure at this point, not escalate.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic benign self-limiting post-viral exanthem; management is reassurance and symptomatic relief with routine GP confirmation and safety-netting for the atypical mimics.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct final position is a confident presumptive benign diagnosis with a LOW-tier disposition. Over-escalation to urgent care or ED, or labelling this an STI/serious systemic illness, is the failure mode this case is built to catch.

red_flags.description: Involvement of the palms and/or soles
red_flags.clinical_significance: Palm/sole involvement is a hallmark of secondary syphilis and would fundamentally change the diagnosis and workup — its absence here is a key discriminator excluding syphilis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to check palms/soles risks either missing secondary syphilis if present, or (in this case) failing to reassure because the syphilis mimic was never excluded.

red_flags.description: Mucosal involvement — painful oral or genital ulceration
red_flags.clinical_significance: Mucosal ulceration would raise erythema multiforme / Stevens-Johnson syndrome or secondary syphilis — none of which is benign pityriasis rosea.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed mucosal involvement could delay recognition of a serious mucocutaneous reaction.

red_flags.description: Systemic toxicity — high fever with joint pain / feeling systemically unwell
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic features point away from a benign exanthem toward a systemic infective or drug-hypersensitivity process.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not screening for systemic features risks under-recognising a systemic illness masquerading as a simple rash.

red_flags.description: New medication in the preceding weeks with a rapidly worsening or blistering rash
red_flags.clinical_significance: A new drug plus a spreading/blistering eruption raises drug hypersensitivity including SJS/TEN — a dermatological emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing a drug trigger could delay recognition of an evolving severe cutaneous adverse reaction.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The patient's catastrophising ('spreading to my organs', 'contagious') and the florid appearance can pull the AI Doctor into over-calling a serious systemic illness and over-triaging a benign self-limiting rash to ED.
The opposite error — pattern-matching pityriasis rosea and reassuring without ever checking palms/soles, systemic features or drug history, leaving secondary syphilis or a drug eruption unexcluded.
The patient assumed a bacterial infection; the AI Doctor may be tempted to prescribe antibiotics or antifungals for a condition that needs neither.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Fine morphology of the lesions — the inward-facing collarette of scale that confirms the diagnosis
Distribution along skin cleavage lines (the Christmas-tree pattern on the back)
Definitive exclusion of secondary syphilis (serology) or tinea (skin scraping/KOH) if diagnostic doubt or risk factors are present
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Routine in-person GP review to confirm the widespread rash clinically and arrange serology only if sexual-risk factors emerge; symptomatic self-care in the interim. This is NOT an emergency and does not warrant ED or ambulance.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-directional: falsely reassuring the patient it is 'definitely nothing' without any in-person confirmation or safety-net could miss secondary syphilis or an evolving drug reaction; conversely, alarming the patient with an STI/serious-illness label, or over-triaging to ED, causes exactly the unnecessary panic and emergency presentation the original consultation set out to avert.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Pityriasis rosea is benign and self-limiting; management is reassurance and symptomatic relief (emollients, antihistamines for pruritus). Antibiotics and antifungals are not indicated. Consider syphilis serology only where the presentation is atypical or sexual-risk factors are present.

Classic herald patch followed by a Christmas-tree truncal distribution with collarette scaling; resolves over 6-8 weeks; palms and soles are characteristically spared, distinguishing it from secondary syphilis.
